perturbation.original_text: The Company, the MHC and the Bank have good and marketable title to all real property and good title to all other assets material to the business of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, taken as a whole, and to those properties and assets described in the Registration Statement and Prospectus as owned by them, free and clear of all liens, charges, encumbrances or restrictions, except such as are described in the Registration Statement and Prospectus, or are not material to the business of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, taken as a whole; and all of the leases and subleases material to the business of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, taken as a whole, under which the Company, the MHC or the Bank hold properties, including those described in the Registration Statement and Prospectus, are in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company, the MHC and the Bank have demonstrable ownership of all real property and acceptable possession of all other assets related to the operations of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, considered collectively. These include properties and assets documented in the Registration Statement and Prospectus as possessions, generally unburdened by claims, debts, or constraints, except those detailed in the Registration Statement and Prospectus, or those insignificant to the business of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, viewed collectively; and all leases and subleases necessary for the business of the Company, the MHC and the Bank, viewed collectively, under which the Company, the MHC, or the Bank maintain properties, inclusive of those detailed in the Registration Statement and Prospectus, are valid and in operation.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'good and marketable title' with 'demonstrable ownership' and 'acceptable possession,' and replaced 'free and clear of all liens, charges, encumbrances or restrictions' with 'generally unburdened by claims, debts, or constraints'. This introduces ambiguity, as 'good and marketable title' is a clearly defined legal term, particularly in real estate law, while 'demonstrable ownership' and 'acceptable possession' are not. Similarly, the phrase 'generally unburdened' is less definitive than 'free and clear of all liens, charges, encumbrances or restrictions'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Real Estate Title Law and the concept of 'Marketable Title'
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, but generally defined in state property codes and common law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from 'good and marketable title' to 'demonstrable ownership' weakens the assurance of clear property rights, contradicting the legal standard for marketable title, which requires a title free from reasonable doubt and readily transferable. State real estate laws define specific requirements for marketable title, which this vague language undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: marketable title | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you marketable title Marketable title is a concept in property law referring to a title free from any claims or disputes about the ownership , or from any threat of litigation . When a seller is selling property to a buyer , an implied promise is that the seller will deliver marketable title to the buyer. A marketable title may also be referred to as good title , merchantable title, or clear title . Various encumbrances upon a piece of land can make the title unmarketable including mortgages , adverse possession claims, and violations of zoning laws. To guard against the possibility of issues in title, many home buyers purchase title insurance , although the protection offered by these policies is limited. It is a good and clear title, without adverse claims against it that would cloud the title. Marketable title allows the owner to sell the property at fair market value without objection from the buyer. To ensure marketable title, a title search can be performed before purchasing real property, to investigate the existence of encumbrances such as mortgages, real estate taxes, liens for sewers, roadways, sidewalks, and other municipal improvements, federal taxes, government claims, legal judgments, foreclosures, condemnations, covenants, and easements. perturbation.justification: The change from 'good and marketable title' to 'demonstrable ownership' weakens the assurance of clear property rights, creating a legal contradiction. 'Marketable title' as defined in both scraped law snippets, refers to a title 'free from any claims or disputes about the ownership' and 'reasonably free of defects and liens that would subject it to a lawsuit.' The changed text uses the terms 'demonstrable ownership' and 'acceptable possession' which are ambiguous and do not guarantee the same level of protection as 'good and marketable title', thus failing to ensure a title free from reasonable doubt. The phrase 'generally unburdened' is less definitive than 'free and clear of all liens, charges, encumbrances or restrictions' providing less assurance and is therefore a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The deposit accounts of the Bank are insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits; and no proceedings for the termination or revocation of such insurance are pending or, to the best knowledge of the Company or the Bank, threatened.
perturbation.changed_text: The deposit accounts of the Bank are insured, with the extent of such insurance subject to ongoing administrative reviews; and no active legal challenges to the continuation of this insurance coverage are underway, to the best understanding of the Company or the Bank.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces "insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits" with "insured, with the extent of such insurance subject to ongoing administrative reviews" and removes clarity regarding the FDIC insurance limits. It also weakens the assurance by stating "no active legal challenges" instead of "no proceedings for the termination or revocation...are pending or threatened".
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 12 U.S.C. § 1821, which establishes the FDIC's role in providing deposit insurance up to a specific limit ($250,000 per depositor, per insured bank). The changed text introduces uncertainty about the extent of coverage, deviating from the clear mandate of the statute.

(B) Net amount of insured deposit The net amount due to any depositor at an insured depository institution shall not exceed the standard maximum deposit insurance amount as determined in accordance
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 12 U.S.C. § 1821 by introducing uncertainty about the extent of deposit insurance coverage. The original text clearly states that deposit accounts are insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits. The modified text replaces this with a statement that the insurance extent is 'subject to ongoing administrative reviews,' which deviates from the clear mandate of the statute, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'The Corporation shall insure the deposits of all insured depository institutions as provided in this chapter' and 'The net amount due to any depositor at an insured depository institution shall not exceed the standard maximum deposit insurance amount'. The revised text introduces ambiguity, undermining the explicit insurance limits mandated by law.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall register its Common Stock under Section 12(g) of the 1934 Act on or prior to the Closing Date pursuant to the Plan and shall request that such registration be effective prior to or upon completion of the Offering. The Company shall maintain the effectiveness of such registration for not less than three years or such shorter period as may be required by the OTS.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company intends to potentially register its Common Stock in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, contingent on future determinations, around the Closing Date, if considered strategically beneficial and will aim to maintain it as long as seen as advantageous.
perturbation.explanation: This replaces a clear obligation to register under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 with a discretionary intention. This introduces uncertainty and weakens the commitment to regulatory compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts the intent of Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. § 78l(g)), which mandates registration for companies meeting certain asset and shareholder thresholds. By making registration discretionary, the modified text undermines the regulatory requirements for transparency and investor protection under federal securities law.

Registration requirements for securities (a) General requirement of registration It shall be unlawful for any member, broker, or dealer to effect any transaction in any security (other than an exempted security) on a national securities exchange unless a registration is effective as to such security for such exchange in accordance with the provisions of this chapter and the rules and regulations thereunder. The provi
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates registration of Common Stock under Section 12(g) of the 1934 Act. The changed text replaces this clear obligation with a discretionary intention to 'potentially register,' contingent on future determinations. This directly contradicts the general requirement of registration as outlined in 15 USC 78l, which states, 'It shall be unlawful for any member, broker, or dealer to effect any transaction in any security...unless a registration is effective.' The modified text introduces uncertainty and weakens the commitment to regulatory compliance, undermining the mandatory nature of the law.
